variables.content: The many-body FEP-DMC calculation of the polaron quasiparticle dispersion in crystalline anatase TiO2 yields a polaron band that is noticeably flatter than the single-particle electronic quasiparticle dispersion obtained from a G0W0 calculation (where G0W0 denotes one-shot GW quasiparticle corrections on top of density functional theory), and extracting effective masses from the curvature of these dispersions along the Γ–X high-symmetry direction gives a polaron effective-mass m_p that is larger than the electronic effective-mass m_e with an enhancement factor m_p/m_e = 1.6, consistent with intermediate electron–phonon renormalization characteristic of a large, dispersive polaron.

Cesium lead bromide (CsPbBr_{3}) undergoes crystallographic phase changes with temperature and adopts an orthorhombic ground-state structure at room temperature, yet ARPES band dispersions measured at room temperature can in practice resemble higher-symmetry cubic-phase dispersions for some momentum-space cuts; therefore using an orthorhombic-structure G_{0}W_{0} calculation that yields m_{h}^{*}=0.226\,m_{0} for direct quantitative comparison with room-temperature ARPES data assumes that the orthorhombic theoretical band structure is an appropriate representation of the electronic states probed in the ARPES experiment, and further assumes that any difference in structural symmetry between the theoretical model and the actual electronic-state symmetry sampled by ARPES does not change the effective mass by an amount comparable to the experimental–theoretical difference being evaluated.

ARPES single-particle spectral-function measurements at finite experimental energy resolution produce a quasiparticle peak whose linewidth is determined by combined instrumental broadening, thermal disorder, and intrinsic many-body scattering; in CsPbBr$_3$ the relevant optical longitudinal-optical (LO) phonon energies are \\leq25\\ \\mathrm{meV} and phonon-replica satellites separated by these energies cannot be resolved because the experimental quasiparticle linewidth exceeds the mode energy separations, yet the ARPES-measured quasiparticle dispersion and the fitted increase in effective mass still faithfully reflect the quasiparticle mass renormalization due to electron–phonon interactions, so that extraction of an increased effective mass from the fitted dispersion constitutes a valid signature of polaronic mass dressing even in the absence of resolved replica peaks.

The exciton binding energy E_B computed as E_B = E_g^dir − E_o, where E_g^dir is the direct G0W0 quasiparticle gap and E_o is the lowest BSE excitonic-peak energy, assumes that polaronic (electron–phonon) renormalization of quasiparticle energies does not substantially change the energetic reference for comparing bound excitons to charge-separated polaron states; in other words, using the G0W0 direct gap as the unbound electron–hole reference without explicit inclusion of electron–phonon induced gap renormalization is taken to yield a valid comparison for exciton-versus-polaron energetics.

For the photoexcited carriers in $CsPbBr_{3}$ nanocrystal thin films, the carrier effective mass renormalization due to polaron formation is characterized by a polaron mass $m_{p}$ equal to $3.5$ times the bare band effective mass $m^{*}$, with the bare mass taken as $m^{*}=0.21\,m_{e}$ where $m_{e}$ is the free-electron rest mass ($m_{e}=9.10938356\times10^{-31}\,$kg); explicitly, $m_{p}=3.5\cdot 0.21\cdot m_{e}$. This multiplicative factor is adopted from prior theoretical and experimental studies of related lead-halide perovskites and is assumed to be applicable under the present sample morphology, temperature, and excitation conditions.
      See cited literature providing polaron mass estimates used by the authors.

Applying the Feynman variational polaron-model weak-to-intermediate coupling expansion
      m_{\\mathrm{pol}}=m_{0}\\left(1+\\frac{\\alpha}{6}+0.025\\alpha^{2}+\\cdots\\right),
      with the inputs m_{0}=0.17\\,m_e and \\alpha=1.81 produces a polaron-renormalized hole mass m_{\\mathrm{pol}}=0.24\\,m_e; this theoretical mass renormalization reduces the discrepancy between the computed bare mass and the ARPES-derived mass to within the experimental uncertainty of \\pm0.02\\,m_e.

Polaron‑parameter calculations performed with variational polaron theory and material dielectric and effective‑mass inputs yield an estimated polaron radius r_p ≈ 36 Å for CsPbBr3; using the approximate mapping between polaron spatial extent and the carrier‑density threshold for overlap n_{\mathrm{overlap}}\sim 1/r_p^3 (modulo order‑unity packing factors), the larger r_p in CsPbBr3 reduces n_{\mathrm{overlap}} and thereby increases the propensity for spatial overlap of distinct polarons and for emitted optical phonons from different polarons to be reabsorbed, which enhances the density‑dependent hot‑phonon bottleneck observed experimentally in CsPbBr3.

Single-shot $G_{0}W_{0}$ quasiparticle calculations performed on top of Kohn–Sham orbitals obtained from the HSE06 hybrid density functional including spin–orbit coupling (SOC) and with a tuned fraction $\alpha$ of exact Hartree–Fock exchange produce direct bandgaps (conduction-band minimum and valence-band maximum located at the $R$-point of the cubic Brillouin zone, $R=(0.5,0.5,0.5)$) of $E_{g}=2.37\ \mathrm{eV}$ for MAPbBr$_{3}$ with $\alpha=50\%$, $E_{g}=2.27\ \mathrm{eV}$ for FAPbBr$_{3}$ with $\alpha=40\%$, and $E_{g}=2.30\ \mathrm{eV}$ for CsPbBr$_{3}$ with $\alpha=55\%$; parabolic fits to the $G_{0}W_{0}$ quasiparticle band dispersions including SOC in small $k$-space regions around the band extrema yield electron and hole effective masses whose harmonic combination $\mu^{-1}=m_{e}^{*-1}+m_{h}^{*-1}$ gives reduced masses $\mu$ (in units of the free-electron mass $m_{0}$) of $\mu=0.113$ for MAPbBr$_{3}$, $\mu=0.115$ for FAPbBr$_{3}$, and $\mu=0.080$ for CsPbBr$_{3}$.

In room-temperature angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) measurements on freshly cleaved single crystals of CsPbBr3, the hole effective mass at the valence-band maximum (VBM) located at the bulk Brillouin-zone R point was determined by fitting local quadratic dispersions and found to be m_{h}^{*}=0.203\pm0.016\,m_{0}, where m_{0} denotes the free-electron mass; this reported value is the average of four independent measurements on four different cleaved samples that produced individual fitted masses of 0.19\,m_{0}, 0.23\,m_{0}, 0.20\,m_{0}, and 0.19\,m_{0}. The identification of the VBM at the bulk R point was made by photon-energy-dependent ARPES mapping of the perpendicular crystal momentum k_{\perp}, and under the free-electron final-state mapping k_{\perp}=\sqrt{\tfrac{2m_{0}}{\hbar^{2}}\bigl(E_{\mathrm{kin}}\cos^{2}\theta + V_{0}\bigr)} with inner potential parameter V_{0}=5.3\ \mathrm{eV} (where \hbar is the reduced Planck constant, E_{\mathrm{kin}} the photoelectron kinetic energy referenced to the vacuum level, and \theta the electron emission polar angle), the first R point in these measurements corresponds to photon energy h\nu=29\ \mathrm{eV}.

A Fröhlich-type large-polaron mass renormalization of approximately 50% applied to the orthorhombic one-shot G_{0}W_{0} bare-band hole mass m_{h}^{*}=0.226\,m_{0} would increase the mass to about 0.339\,m_{0}; given the experimentally measured hole mass 0.203\pm0.016\,m_{0} reported here, such a 50% enhancement would exceed the experimental value by roughly 0.136\,m_{0}, which corresponds to more than eight times the quoted experimental standard deviation, and therefore a 50% polaronic renormalization of that magnitude would be readily detectable as a statistically significant discrepancy between the ARPES measurement and the orthorhombic G_{0}W_{0} bare-band reference under the present uncertainty assumptions.
